name: Titanium
name_origin: Named by Martin Heinrich Klaproth in 1795 after the Titans of Greek mythology - the powerful sons of the Earth goddess Gaia - reflecting its strength.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Titanium is the ninth most abundant element in Earth's crust (~0.57% by mass) and the seventh most abundant metal. It is found in nearly all igneous rocks and sediments, primarily as the minerals rutile (TiO2) and ilmenite (FeTiO3).

compounds.name: Titanium dioxide
compounds.summary: A bright white pigment used in paints, sunscreens, food coloring, and as a photocatalyst.

compounds.name: Titanium tetrachloride
compounds.summary: A volatile colorless liquid used as an intermediate in titanium metal production and in Ziegler-Natta polymerization catalysts.

compounds.name: Titanium nitride
compounds.summary: An extremely hard ceramic used as a gold-colored coating on cutting tools, drill bits, and decorative finishes.

compounds.name: Titanium carbide
compounds.summary: A very hard refractory ceramic used in tool bits, heat-resistant coatings, and tungsten carbide composites.

compounds.name: Titanium(IV) isopropoxide
compounds.summary: An organotitanium compound widely used as a catalyst and as a precursor for thin-film titanium oxides.

uses: Aerospace structural components (jet engines, airframes) due to high strength-to-weight ratio
Medical implants such as hip joints and dental implants for biocompatibility
Titanium dioxide pigment in paints, plastics, paper, and sunscreen
Marine and chemical equipment due to corrosion resistance, especially to seawater
Sporting goods (golf clubs, bicycle frames) and consumer electronics housings
reactivity_summary: Titanium is a strong, lightweight transition metal with excellent corrosion resistance due to a passive oxide film. At room temperature it resists most acids, chlorine, and seawater, but at elevated temperatures it reacts readily with oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, and halogens. Powdered titanium is pyrophoric and can burn in pure nitrogen.
isotopes_summary: Titanium has five stable isotopes: 46Ti, 47Ti, 48Ti (most abundant at ~73.7%), 49Ti, and 50Ti. Radioactive isotopes include 44Ti (half-life ~60 years), which is produced in supernovae and used in astrophysical studies.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
